summary: Gruber explains how to enable Apple Watch 'fully charged' notifications in WatchOS 7, a feature he lost when switching from a Series 6 review unit back to his personal Series 5. The setting is buried in the Sleep section of the Apple Watch app, but only appears after you've set up Sleep tracking in the Health app — a non-obvious prerequisite. He walks through the workaround: enable Sleep in Health, then disable all the sleep features you don't want, leaving just the battery notification toggle. The post highlights a real usability problem where a useful, simple feature is gated behind an unrelated multi-step setup process.
key_points: The 'fully charged' notification setting only appears in the Apple Watch app after you enable Sleep tracking in the Health app — a hidden prerequisite
The feature is filed under 'Sleep' settings, which doesn't make intuitive sense for a battery notification
The workaround requires enabling Sleep features you don't want just to reveal the battery notification toggle, then disabling everything else
Gruber prefers David Smith's Sleep++ app for passive sleep tracking over Apple's built-in sleep features, which require schedule-setting and active management
money_quotes: The problem is, if you haven't set up your watch to track sleep in the Health app, the setting for these battery notifications doesn't appear in the Apple Watch app.
It's all a bit confusing, because I don't think it makes intuitive sense that these notifications are filed under 'Sleep'.
Nor that you need to set up Sleep in one app (Health) to get features enabled in another app (Apple Watch).
I never would have figured this out on my own.
It feels a bit convoluted that the solution involved me turning on a bunch of Sleep features I didn't want to get a battery notification setting to appear.

What I love about using Sleep++ is that it's entirely passive. I don't set a schedule, Sleep++ doesn't tell me when to go to sleep, and I don't have to do anything when I go to bed other than just wear my Apple Watch while I sleep.
key_insight: Apple buried a universally useful battery notification behind an unrelated Sleep setup process, creating a usability problem where a simple feature requires navigating two apps and enabling unwanted features just to reveal a toggle.
